places.name: Croatian National Theatre (HNK Zagreb)
places.description: Neo-baroque opera house on Trg maršala Tita, with a mint-green dome and gilded auditorium that anchors the city's cultural identity.
places.local_story: Croats call it simply HNK; premiere nights still feel like city-wide events, and many families have stories of grandparents who queued for standing tickets. The facade is what you photograph, but locals brag about the acoustics inside.
places.practical_tip: Walk the full perimeter for the best dome shots; check hnk.hr for a guided tour or a ballet/opera ticket if you want to see the interior.
highlights: Frescoes and chandelier in the auditorium
Location between Zrinjevac and the main square axis

places.name: Zagreb Cathedral (Katedrala Marijina Uznesenja)
places.description: Gothic-revival twin spires above Kaptol — the tallest church in Croatia and the visual anchor of the historic upper town.
places.local_story: The spires were damaged in the 2020 earthquake and the scaffolding became part of daily life for years; restoration made headlines because the cathedral is the symbol of Zagreb itself. Even skeptics admit the silhouette from Dolac is unforgettable.
places.practical_tip: Circle the exterior first for gargoyles and portal detail, then step inside for the nave height; combine with the short walk to Dolac market above.
highlights: Kaptol square setting and views toward Upper Town

places.name: Palace Hotel (facade)
places.description: Secession-era hotel on Trg bana Jelačića with a richly ornamented corner facade and one of the liveliest people-watching perches in the city.
places.local_story: Locals meet 'ispod konja' (under the horse statue) and the Palace is the backdrop to half those rendezvous; the building reads as old Zagreb commerce and cafe society in one block.
places.practical_tip: Study the floral reliefs and curved bay windows from across the square at golden hour; the ground-floor cafe is a classic stop for kava.
highlights: Corner site on the main square
Historic hotel continuity since the early 20th century

places.name: Art Pavilion (Umjetnički paviljon)
places.description: Yellow Secession temple at the south end of Zrinjevac — purpose-built for exhibitions under a vast glass dome.
places.local_story: Built for the 1898 Budapest millennium exhibition and reassembled here, it still feels like a small palace for art rather than a white-cube gallery. The colour against the park is a postcard cliché because it actually works.
places.practical_tip: Walk Zrinjevac toward the pavilion for the classic perspective; buy a ticket when a show interests you, but the exterior alone rewards architecture fans.

places.name: Meštrović Pavilion (HDLU)
places.description: Circular Ivan Meštrović design on Trg žrtava fašizma — stripped classical forms around a colonnade, originally conceived as an art pavilion.
places.local_story: People still argue whether to call the square by its older or current name; the building itself is a quiet flex of Croatian modern sculpture sensibility translated into architecture. Exhibition openings here feel deliberately civic.
places.practical_tip: Circle the colonnade for changing light on the stone; compare its discipline to the ornate Art Pavilion fifty metres away.
highlights: Meštrović's architectural language in the round
Contrast with the Art Pavilion across the green

places.name: Secession facades, Masarykova ulica
places.description: A stretch of early-20th-century apartment and commercial buildings with floral stucco, cartouches, and curved glass — downtown Secession without the museum queue.
places.local_story: Masarykova is a commuter artery, so locals rush past these facades daily; architecture nerds slow down between meetings to read the stucco. It is the anti-tourist route compared to the main square loop.
places.practical_tip: Walk the block between Trg bana Jelačića and the theatre district, looking up at balconies and mascarons; go early before shop awnings hide the upper floors.
highlights: Link between the square and HNK/Zrinjevac

places.name: Museum of Contemporary Art (MSU)
places.description: Igor Franić's elongated concrete-and-glass museum on Dubrovnik Avenue — the city's flagship brutalist volume and home of the contemporary collection.
places.local_story: Novi Zagreb residents treat MSU as proof their side of the river has serious culture, not just tower blocks; the building's scale reads as a deliberate counterpoint to the historic centre's ornament.
places.practical_tip: Take the tram across the Sava for the approach along the building's full length; combine with the roof terrace and a ticketed show if you want the full interior experience.
highlights: Brutalist massing and long facade rhythm
Major Croatian and international contemporary works inside
